perturbation.original_text: 4.13 Independent Contractor. Vendor hereby represents and warrants to AT&T that: (a) Vendor is engaged in an independent business and will perform all obligations under this Agreement as an independent contractor and not as the agent or employee of AT&T; (b) Vendor's personnel performing Services shall be considered solely the employees or agents of Vendor and not employees or agents of AT&T; (c) Vendor has and retains the right to exercise full control of and supervision over the performance of the Services and full control over the employment, direction, assignment, compensation and discharge of all personnel performing the Services; and (d) Vendor is solely responsible for all matters relating to compensation and benefits for all of Vendor's personnel who perform Services.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.13 Independent Contractor. Vendor hereby represents and warrants to AT&T that: (a) Vendor is engaged in an independent business and will perform all obligations under this Agreement as an independent contractor but may also be considered a worker and not solely as the agent or employee of AT&T; (b) Vendor's personnel performing Services shall be considered workers and agents of both Vendor and AT&T; (c) Vendor has and retains the right to exercise some control of and supervision over the performance of the Services, while AT&T retains final control over the project; and (d) Vendor and AT&T share responsibility for all matters relating to compensation and benefits for all of Vendor's personnel who perform Services.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the definition of 'Independent Contractor' to imply a joint employment or worker status, directly contradicting the explicit independent contractor declaration. This could cause legal ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA defines 'employee' broadly, including individuals 'suffered or permitted to work.' The changed text implies AT&T permits and controls Vendor's personnel, potentially classifying them as employees under the FLSA, thus contradicting the independent contractor designation.

The Section 14(c) Advisor helps users understand the special minimum wage requirements for workers with disabilities. perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the independent contractor designation by implying a joint employment relationship between the Vendor, AT&T and the Vendor's personnel. Specifically, the revised text states that Vendor's personnel are 'workers and agents of both Vendor and AT&T,' and that 'Vendor and AT&T share responsibility for all matters relating to compensation and benefits.' This directly conflicts with the original text which explicitly states that the Vendor's personnel are solely employees or agents of the Vendor, and that the Vendor is solely responsible for all compensation and benefits matters. The perturbation explanation states that the modification changes the definition of independent contractor and implies joint employment status, which causes legal ambiguity. The scraped law snippet, while not directly defining 'independent contractor,' mentions violations related to recordkeeping and monetary statutes, implying a regulatory framework that distinguishes between employer-employee relationships and other arrangements, which the changed text obscures.

perturbation.original_text: 3.15 Indemnity (a) Except for Covered Losses that are exclusively provided for in the Section entitled "Infringement," Vendor shall indemnify, hold harmless, and defend the AT&T Indemnified Parties, in accordance with this Section, against any Third Party Loss arising from, or in connection with, or resulting from, (i) any default, violation, breach or nonperformance by Vendor or any Vendor Person of Vendor's representations, warranties, covenants and obligations under this Agreement, the Build Addendum or the Maintenance Addendum, (ii) the Deliverables, Material or Services furnished by Vendor or any Vendor Person and the use, construction, maintenance, operation or occupancy of any Cell Site by Vendor or any Vendor Person, (iii) the negligent or willful acts or omissions of Vendor and each Vendor Person with respect to this Agreement or (iv) Employment Claims. Subject to AT&T's obligation to reimburse or indemnify Vendor as set forth in Subsection (c) or (d) below, respectively, Vendor's duty to indemnify, hold harmless, and defend the AT&T Indemnified Parties against any Third Party Loss extends to any Third Party Loss that may be caused or alleged to be caused in part by the negligence of any of the AT&T Indemnified Parties to the fullest extent that such indemnification is permitted by applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.15 Indemnity (a) Except for Covered Losses that are exclusively provided for in the Section entitled "Infringement," Vendor shall indemnify, hold harmless, and defend the AT&T Indemnified Parties, in accordance with this Section, against any Third Party Loss arising from, or in connection with, or resulting from, (i) any default, violation, breach or nonperformance by Vendor or any Vendor Person of Vendor's representations, warranties, covenants and obligations under this Agreement, the Build Addendum or the Maintenance Addendum, (ii) the Deliverables, Material or Services furnished by Vendor or any Vendor Person and the use, construction, maintenance, operation or occupancy of any Cell Site by Vendor or any Vendor Person, (iii) the grossly negligent or willful acts or omissions of Vendor and each Vendor Person with respect to this Agreement or (iv) Employment Claims. Subject to AT&T's obligation to reimburse or indemnify Vendor as set forth in Subsection (c) or (d) below, respectively, Vendor's duty to indemnify, hold harmless, and defend the AT&T Indemnified Parties against any Third Party Loss extends to any Third Party Loss that may be caused or alleged to be caused in part by the ordinary negligence of any of the AT&T Indemnified Parties to the fullest extent that such indemnification is permitted by applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text changes 'negligent or willful acts or omissions' to 'grossly negligent or willful acts or omissions,' but retains the clause where Vendor indemnifies AT&T for *ordinary* negligence. This creates a legal contradiction because AT&T can be indemnified for ordinary negligence but Vendor is only liable for gross negligence. This creates a term that is not compliant with contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law requires clear and unambiguous language. The conflicting standards of negligence create ambiguity and potentially render the indemnity clause unenforceable due to conflicting interpretations of negligence standards.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by altering the negligence standard for Vendor's liability while maintaining AT&T's indemnification coverage for ordinary negligence. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that the Vendor is only liable for gross negligence, but AT&T is indemnified even for ordinary negligence. The Law Explanation further emphasizes that contract law requires clear and unambiguous language. The change undermines the clarity of the contract because the modified version sets a higher bar for Vendor's negligence liability (gross negligence) while still requiring them to indemnify AT&T for ordinary negligence, thereby creating an imbalance and potential ambiguity that makes the terms difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: 2.18 Excusable Delay. "Excusable Delay" means a delay in Vendor's performance or obligations with respect to any Cell Site past any scheduled Delivery Date or Completion Date (including any scheduled Completion Date for a Milestone, scheduled Completion Date for Location Acceptance or scheduled Phase Completion Date set forth in the Build Addendum) that is caused by any of the following events: (a) a Force Majeure Event affecting Vendor's performance with respect to such Cell Site, subject to Section 3.12(b); (b) a Change Order that extends a Completion Date or Delivery Date with respect to such Cell Site; (c) a Permitting Delay affecting such Cell Site, subject to the process set forth in Section 3.3(b); (d) AT&T's failure to deliver the AT&T Provided Equipment (as such term is defined in the Build Addendum) within sixty (60) days prior to the applicable Phase Completion Date for such Cell Site; (e) any material defect, insufficiency, error or deficiency in any AT&T Provided Equipment or other items supplied by AT&T under this Agreement; or (f) AT&T's failure to satisfy any of the AT&T Task Service Level Agreements within the time periods set forth on Schedule 14 to the Build Addendum. Upon the occurrence of any of the triggering events set forth in clauses (a) through (f) causing a delay, such delay shall only be an Excusable Delay for the number of days that such triggering event was in existence and the applicable Delivery Date or Completion Date shall be extended on a day-for-day basis for the length of such triggering event.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.18 Excusable Delay. "Excusable Delay" means a delay in Vendor's performance or obligations with respect to any Cell Site past any scheduled Delivery Date or Completion Date (including any scheduled Completion Date for a Milestone, scheduled Completion Date for Location Acceptance or scheduled Phase Completion Date set forth in the Build Addendum) that is caused by any of the following events: (a) a Force Majeure Event affecting Vendor's performance with respect to such Cell Site, subject to Section 3.12(b); (b) a Change Order that extends a Completion Date or Delivery Date with respect to such Cell Site; (c) a Permitting Delay affecting such Cell Site, subject to the process set forth in Section 3.3(b); (d) AT&T's failure to deliver the AT&T Provided Equipment (as such term is defined in the Build Addendum) within sixty (60) days prior to the applicable Phase Completion Date for such Cell Site; (e) any material defect, insufficiency, error or deficiency in any AT&T Provided Equipment or other items supplied by AT&T under this Agreement; or (f) AT&T's failure to satisfy any of the AT&T Task Service Level Agreements within the time periods set forth on Schedule 14 to the Build Addendum. Upon the occurrence of any of the triggering events set forth in clauses (a) through (f) causing a delay, such delay shall be considered an Excusable Delay, and the applicable Delivery Date or Completion Date shall be extended at AT&T's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition mandates a day-for-day extension. The modified definition makes the extension discretionary for AT&T, contradicting the previously defined 'Excusable Delay'. This introduces uncertainty into the contract terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under UCC 2-209, modifications to contracts for the sale of goods require good faith. Making the extension discretionary, while the delay is labeled 'excusable', could be interpreted as a lack of good faith, especially if AT&T unreasonably withholds extensions for legitimate delays caused by the specified events, thus disrupting Vendor's reasonable expectations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. (1) An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding. (2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded, but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party. (3) The requirements of the statute of frauds section of this Article (Section 2-201 ) must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (4)Although an attempt at modification or rescission does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (2) or (3) it can operate as a waiver. (5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term waived, unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change of position in reliance on the waiver. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction because it changes a mandatory obligation ('shall be extended on a day-for-day basis') into a discretionary one ('shall be extended at AT&T's sole discretion'). This shift undermines the Vendor's reasonable expectation of an extension for excusable delays, potentially conflicting with the good faith requirement for contract modifications, as highlighted in the Law Explanation referencing UCC 2-209. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (UCC 2-209) does not explicitly state 'good faith,' the Law Explanation draws a reasonable inference that discretionary extension for excusable delays may violate good faith provisions if AT&T unreasonably withholds extensions. The original text clearly defines 'excusable delay' and its consequence (day-for-day extension), while the changed text introduces ambiguity and allows for AT&T to deny the extension. This misalignment and potential for abuse creates a contradiction.
